elements.sent-2411-001: In the present paper we study $\eta$-Ricci solitons on Kenmotsu 3-manifolds.
elements.sent-2411-002: Moreover, we consider $\eta$-Ricci solitons on Kenmotsu 3-manifolds with Codazzi type of Ricci tensor and cyclic parallel Ricci tensor.
elements.sent-2411-003: Beside these, we study $\phi$-Ricci symmetric $\eta$-Ricci soliton on Kenmotsu 3-manifolds.
elements.sent-2411-004: Also Kenmotsu 3-manifolds satisfying the curvature condition $R.R=Q(S,R)$ is considered.
elements.sent-2411-005: Finally, an example is constructed to prove the existence of a proper $\eta$-Ricci soliton on a Kenmotsu 3-manifold.
elements.sent-2411-006: In modern mathematics, the methods of contact geometry play an important role.
elements.sent-2411-007: Contact geometry has evolved from the mathematical formalism of classical mechanics.
elements.sent-2411-008: In the present paper we are entering an era of new concepts, some generalizations and applications which play a functional role in contemporary mathematics.
elements.sent-2411-009: The product of an almost contact manifold $M$ and the real line $\mathbb {R}$ carries a natural almost complex structure.
elements.sent-2411-010: However, if one takes $M$ to be an almost contact metric manifold and suppose that the product metric $G$ on $M\times \mathbb {R}$ is Kaehlerian, then the structure on $M$ is cosymplectic \cite{ian and not Sasakian.}
elements.sent-2411-011: On the other hand, Oubina \cite{oub pointed out that if the conformally related metric $e^{2t G$, $t$ being the coordinate on $\mathbb {R}$, is Kaehlerian, then $M$ is Sasakian and conversely.\par In \cite{tan S.}}}
elements.sent-2411-012: Tanno classified almost contact metric manifolds whose automorphism groups possess the maximum dimension.
elements.sent-2411-013: For such a manifold $M$, the sectional curvature of plane section containing $\xi$ is a constant, say $c$.
elements.sent-2411-014: If $c>0$, $M$ is a homogeneous Sasakian manifold of constant sectional curvature.
elements.sent-2411-015: If $c=0$, $M$ is the product of a line or a circle with a Kaehler manifold of constant holomorphic sectional curvature.
elements.sent-2411-016: If $c<0$, $M$ is a warped product space $\mathbb {R}\times_{ f C ^{n}$.. In $1972$, K.}
elements.sent-2411-017: Kenmotsu \cite{ken abstracted the differential geometric properties of the third case.}
elements.sent-2411-018: We call it Kenmotsu manifold.\par In 1982, R.. S.
elements.sent-2411-019: Hamilton \cite{hr introduced the notion of Ricci flow to find a canonical metric on a smooth manifold.}
elements.sent-2411-020: The Ricci flow is an evolution equation for metrics on a Riemannian manifold defined as follows: \begin{eqnarray}\frac{\partial}{\partial_t}g_{ij}=-2R_{ij}.\label{a1}\end{eqnarray} Ricci solitons are special solutions of the Ricci flow equation (\ref{a1 ) of the form $g_{ij =\sigma(t)\psi_t^\ast g_{ij}$ with the initial condition $g_{ij (0)=g_{ij}$, where $\psi_t$ are diffeomorphisms of $M$ and $\sigma(t)$ is the scaling function.\par A Ricci soliton is a generalization of an Einstein metric.}}}
elements.sent-2411-021: We recall the notion of Ricci soliton according to \cite{CC .}
elements.sent-2411-022: On the manifold $M$, a Ricci soliton is a triple $(g,V,\lambda )$ with $g$ a Riemannian metric, $V$ a vector field, called potential vector field and $\lambda $ a real scalar such that \begin{eqnarray}\pounds_{V}g+2S+2\lambda g=0,\label{a2}\end{eqnarray} where $\pounds$ is the Lie derivative.
elements.sent-2411-023: Metrics satisfying (\ref{a2 ) are interesting and useful in physics and are often referred as quasi-Einstein (\cite{chave1 ,\cite{chave2 ).\par Theoretical physicists have also been looking into the equation of Ricci soliton in relation with string theory.}}}
elements.sent-2411-024: The initial contribution in this direction is due to Friedan \cite{friedam who discusses some aspects of it.\par The Ricci soliton is said to be shrinking, steady and expanding according as $\lambda $ is negative, zero and positive respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-025: Ricci solitons have been studied by several authors such as (\cite{deshmukh1 ,\cite{deshmukh2 ,\cite{hr ,\cite{dugg ,\cite{it ) and many others.\par As a generalization of Ricci soliton, the notion of $\eta$-Ricci soliton was introduced by Cho and Kimura \cite{cho1 .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-026: This notion has also been studied in \cite{CC for Hopf hypersurfaces in complex space forms.}
elements.sent-2411-027: An $\eta$-Ricci soliton is a tuple $(g,V,\lambda ,\mu )$, where $V$ is a vector field on $M$, $\lambda$ and $\mu$ are constants, and $g$ is a Riemannian (or pseudo-Riemannian) metric satisfying the equation \begin{eqnarray}\pounds_{V}g+2S+2\lambda g+2\mu \eta \otimes \eta =0,\label{a3}\end{eqnarray} where $S$ is the Ricci tensor associated to $g$.
elements.sent-2411-028: In this connection, we mention the works of Blaga (\cite{blaga1 , \cite{blaga2 ) and Prakasha et al. \cite{dg on $\eta$-Ricci solitons.}}}
elements.sent-2411-029: In particular, if $\mu =0$, then the notion of $\eta$-Ricci soliton $(g,V,\lambda ,\mu )$ reduces to the notion of Ricci soliton $(g,V,\lambda )$.
elements.sent-2411-030: If $\mu\not=0$, then the $\eta$-Ricci soliton is named proper $\eta$-Ricci soliton.
elements.sent-2411-031: Motivated by the above works, we study $\eta$-Ricci solitons on Kenmotsu 3-manifolds.
elements.sent-2411-032: The paper is organized as follows:\par After preliminaries in Section 2, we study $\eta$-Ricci solitons on a Kenmotsu 3-manifold.
elements.sent-2411-033: Beside these, we study $\phi$-Ricci symmetric $\eta$-Ricci solitons on Kenmotsu 3-manifolds.
elements.sent-2411-034: In the next section, Kenmotsu 3-manifolds satisfying the curvature condition $R.R=Q(S,R)$ are studied.
elements.sent-2411-035: Let $M$ be a connected almost contact metric manifold with an almost contact metric structure ($\phi$,$\xi$,$\eta$,$g$), that is, $\phi$ is an ($1$,$1$)-tensor field, $\xi$ is a vector field, $\eta$ is a $1$-form and $g$ is a compatible Riemannian metric such that \begin{eqnarray}\label{b1} \phi^{2}(X)=-X +\eta(X)\xi, \eta(\xi)= 1, \phi\xi= 0, \eta\phi = 0\end{eqnarray} \begin{eqnarray}\label{b2} g(\phi X,\phi Y) = g(X,Y) - \eta(X)\eta(Y)\end{eqnarray} \begin{eqnarray}\label{b3} g(X,\xi) = \eta(X)\end{eqnarray} for all $X,Y\in \Gamma (TM)$(\cite{bla1 ,\cite{bla2 ).}}
elements.sent-2411-036: If an almost contact metric manifold satisfies \begin{eqnarray}(\nabla _{X}\phi)Y=g(\phi X,Y)\xi-\eta (Y)\phi X,\label{b4}\end{eqnarray} then $M$ is called a Kenmotsu manifold \cite{ken , where $\nabla$ is the Levi-Civita connection of $g$.}
elements.sent-2411-038: Then the relation $(1.3)$ yields \begin{eqnarray}(\pounds_{\xi }g)(X,Y)+2S(X,Y)+2\lambda g(X,Y)+2\mu \eta (X)\eta (Y)=0 .\label{b13}\end{eqnarray} It follows that \begin{eqnarray}2S(X,Y)=-(\pounds _{\xi }g)(X,Y)-2\lambda g(X,Y)-2\mu \eta (X)\eta (Y),\label{b14}\end{eqnarray} for all smooth vector fields $X,Y\in \Gamma (TM)$.
elements.sent-2411-039: In a Kenmotsu 3-manifold we have \begin{eqnarray}(\pounds _{\xi }g)(X,Y)=2[g(X,Y)-\eta(X)\eta(Y)].\label{b15}\end{eqnarray} Making use of (\ref{b15 ) in (\ref{b14 ) we get \begin{eqnarray}S(X,Y)=-(\lambda+1) g(X,Y)-(\mu-1) \eta (X)\eta (Y)\label{b16}.\end{eqnarray}.}}
elements.sent-2411-040: Comparing the above equation (\ref{b12 ) with (\ref{b16 ), we find $\lambda+1 =-\frac{1 {2 (r+2)$ and $\mu-1 =\frac{1 {2 (6+r),$ from which it follows that $\lambda +\mu =2.$ This completes the proof.}}}}}}
elements.section-2411-003: $\eta$-Ricci Solitons on Kenmotsu 3-manifolds with Coddazi type of Ricci tensor
elements.sent-2411-041: In this section we consider proper $\eta$-Ricci soliton on Kenmotsu 3-manifolds with Coddazi type of Ricci tensor.. A.
elements.sent-2411-042: Gray \cite{gray introduced the notion of cyclic parallel Ricci tensor and Codazzi type of Ricci tensor.}

elements.sent-2411-044: In a 3-dimensional Riemannian manifold, we have \begin{eqnarray}R(X,Y)Z = g(Y,Z)QX-g(X,Z)QY +S(Y,Z)X-S(X,Z)Y \nonumber -\frac{r}{2}[g(Y,Z)X-g(X,Z)Y],\label{c6}\end{eqnarray} where $Q$ is the Ricci operator, that is $g(QX,Y)=S(X,Y)$.
elements.sent-2411-045: Now using the values of $S$, $Q$ and $r$ in the above equation we get \begin{eqnarray}R(X,Y)Z =-[g(Y,Z)X-g(X,Z)Y].\label{c7}\end{eqnarray} Thus we conclude the following: \begin{thm A Kenmotsu 3-manifold with Coddazi type of Ricci tensor admittting a proper $\eta$-Ricci soliton of the type $(g,V,1, 1 )$ is locally isometric to the hyperbolic space H$(-1)$.\end{thm.}}
elements.section-2411-004: $\eta$-Ricci solitons on Kenmotsu 3-manifolds with cyclic parallel Ricci tensor
elements.sent-2411-046: This section is devoted to study proper $\eta$-Ricci solitons on Kenmotsu 3-manifolds with cyclic parallel Ricci tensor.

elements.sent-2411-049: In this section we study $\phi$-Ricci symmetric proper $\eta$-Ricci solitons on Kenmotsu 3-manifolds.
elements.sent-2411-050: A Kenmotsu manifold is said to be $\phi$-Ricci symmetric if \begin{eqnarray}\phi^{2}(\nabla_{X}Q)Y=0,\label{e1}\end{eqnarray} for all smooth vector fields $X$, $Y$.
elements.sent-2411-051: The Ricci tensor for an $\eta$-Ricci soliton on Kenmotsu 3-manifold is given by \begin{eqnarray}S(X,Y)=-(\lambda+1) g(X,Y)-(\mu-1) \eta (X)\eta (Y).\label{e2}\end{eqnarray} Then it follows that \begin{eqnarray}QX=-(\lambda+1) X-(\mu-1) \eta (X)\xi,\label{e3}\end{eqnarray} for all smooth vector fields $X$.

elements.sent-2411-053: Thus we can state the following: \begin{thm Let $(M,\phi, \xi,\eta,g)$ be a Kenmotsu 3-manifold.}
elements.sent-2411-054: If $M$ is $\phi$-Ricci symmetric, then $\mu=1$, $\lambda=1$ and the manifold is locally isometric to the hyperbolic space H$(-1)$.\end{thm.}
elements.sent-2411-055: In this section we deal with $\eta$-Ricci solitons on Kenmotsu 3-manifolds satisfying $R.R=Q(S,R)$.

elements.section-2411-007: Example of a proper $\eta$-Ricci Soliton on a Kenmotsu 3-manifold
elements.sent-2411-071: We consider the 3-dimensional manifold $M=\{(x,y,z)\in \mathbb{R ^{3 , z\neq0\},$ where $(x,y,z)$ are the standard coordinate of $\mathbb{R ^{3 .$ The vector fields\begin{equation}e_{1}=z\frac{\partial }{\partial x}, e_{2}=z\frac{\partial } {\partial y} , e_{3}=-z\frac{\partial }{\partial z}\end{equation} are linearly independent at each point of $M.$ Let $g$ be the Riemannian metric defined by \begin{equation}g(e_{1},e_{3})=g(e_{1},e_{2})=g(e_{2},e_{3})=0,\end{equation} \begin{equation}g(e_{1},e_{1})=g(e_{2},e_{2})=g(e_{3},e_{3})=1.\end{equation}.}}}}

elements.sent-2411-073: Then using the linearity of $\phi $ and $g$, we have \begin{equation}\eta (e_{3})=1,\end{equation} \begin{equation}\phi ^{2}Z=-Z+\eta (Z)e_{3},\end{equation} \begin{equation}g(\phi Z,\phi W)=g(Z,W)-\eta (Z)\eta (W),\end{equation} for any $Z,W\in \Gamma (TM).$ Then for $e_{3 =\xi $, $(\phi ,\xi ,\eta ,g)$ defines an almost contact metric structure on $M$.}
